title: The Immaculate Election

jokes.text: Michael was surprised to find the house still a mess a week after the fumigation — narrator reveals Michael was hiding his father in the attic, making his request to clean up self-sabotaging
jokes.setup: Michael complains the house is still a mess after fumigation and scolds Lindsay for not cleaning up
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals Michael himself was hiding his father in the attic, making the fumigation call dangerous and his complaints hypocritical
jokes.explanation: Classic AD structure: the narrator's dry reveal recontextualizes the entire scene. Michael's moral high ground collapses instantly. The joke rewards attentive viewers with layered irony and is a perfect example of the show's signature narrator-as-comedian technique. Rewatch bonus because on first viewing you miss how completely Michael is hoist by his own petard.

jokes.text: If you weren't all the way on the other side of the room, I'd slap your face.
jokes.setup: Michael is scolding Lindsay for being lazy
jokes.punchline: His threat of physical punishment is undermined by his own admission that he's too lazy to cross the room to carry it out — mirroring Lindsay's laziness
jokes.explanation: Economical and sharp. Michael's complaint about Lindsay's laziness is punctured by his own identical laziness in the same breath, creating self-own irony. High quotability because the line is perfectly constructed — the conditional clause does all the work.

jokes.text: Lindsay intercepting Lupe (her mother's housekeeper) as she arrives for work and taking her to the Bluth house to clean — 'Can I take you the rest of the way? Almost done. Yours... or Mister's?'
jokes.setup: Narrator says Lindsay had only cleaned the house once; we expect a flashback of Lindsay cleaning
jokes.punchline: She 'cleaned' by poaching her mother Lucille's housekeeper mid-journey and having Lupe clean instead — confirmed by Lupe's detail-oriented question about whose mess she's cleaning
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed bit that uses the narrator's setup, a physical flashback, and a perfect closing line ('Lupe never accepted a ride from Lindsay again') to deliver a complete comedic short story in under 30 seconds. Deeply character-specific — this is exactly Lindsay. The 'Yours or Mister's?' detail grounds the absurdity in plausible domestic reality.

jokes.text: You didn't even notice he'd gone, did you? / I did notice we'd stopped TiVoing the Christopher Lowell show.
jokes.setup: Lindsay tells Michael that Tobias moving out hasn't been easy for any of them
jokes.punchline: Michael's sole evidence that he noticed Tobias was gone is the absence of a TiVo recording of an interior decorating show — implicitly revealing Tobias's effeminate interests and Michael's obliviousness
jokes.explanation: Sharp economy — the Christopher Lowell reference (a real interior design TV host) does double duty: it's Michael's oblivious one-data-point 'noticing' and a gentle dig at Tobias's sexuality. Works on multiple levels without belaboring any of them.

jokes.text: Tobias in Lindsay's lingerie: 'Well, excuse me for liking the way they shape my junk.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is wearing Lindsay's underwear; she tells him he's going to stretch them out
jokes.punchline: His defensive response casually mentions 'my junk' — normalizing cross-dressing with a bluntly masculine justification, absurdly blending hyper-masculine language with the context
jokes.explanation: The collision of Tobias's oblivious self-presentation and the crudely masculine 'junk' makes this a classic Tobias line. Could ONLY be Tobias — perfectly character-integrated. High quotability because it works out of context.

jokes.text: You can pack your junk and get out.
jokes.setup: Tobias says the underwear 'shapes my junk'
jokes.punchline: Lindsay uses 'junk' in the double sense — the underwear/body AND his belongings — as her divorce ultimatum
jokes.explanation: A perfectly constructed wordplay payoff to joke #5. The double meaning is set up naturally by Tobias's own word choice, so it feels earned rather than forced. Maximum economy — four words do everything.

jokes.text: You're kicking me out? At the beginning of pilot season?
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just kicked Tobias out of the house
jokes.punchline: Tobias's primary concern about being kicked out is not the marriage ending but the professional timing — 'pilot season' — revealing his delusion about his acting career as more urgent than his marriage
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias priority inversion — his delusional acting career concerns trump his marriage. 'Pilot season' is a real industry term deployed absurdly. Character-specific and efficient.

jokes.text: Tobias takes up residence in Detective Frank Wrench's apartment on the set of the TV show 'Wrench.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has nowhere to go after being kicked out
jokes.punchline: He moves into a fictional TV set apartment as if it were real housing — the narrator delivers this with complete deadpan matter-of-factness
jokes.explanation: The absurdist premise is sold entirely by the narrator's deadpan delivery and the specificity of 'Detective Frank Wrench on the set of the television show: Wrench.' The setup for later jokes about Tobias on a fake set. Rewatch bonus for paying off in the bathroom scene.

jokes.text: Gob's magic video: 'I'm making a magic video. I'm going to tape myself doing tricks around the office. I'm calling it Tricks... let me finish — Around the Office.'
jokes.setup: Gob says he's figured out a way to make money while working
jokes.punchline: His 'idea' is literally just filming himself doing magic tricks at work, which he's pre-defensively protecting with 'let me finish' before finishing with the most obvious possible title completion
jokes.explanation: The 'let me finish' preemption before a completely unsurprising finish is a perfect Gob construction. His grandiosity meets the most trivial content. The beat of him protecting an anti-climax is the joke's engine.

jokes.text: That is what we call working. Something you might want to try next time you're at the office.
jokes.setup: Gob says he's found a way to make money while working
jokes.punchline: Michael points out that what Gob described IS working — a burn delivered with characteristic Michael dry condescension
jokes.explanation: Solid straight-man burn. Michael's dismissal of Gob's 'innovation' as simply 'working' is clean and efficient, but not exceptional — it's the kind of line Michael delivers every episode.

jokes.text: Home video reveals: 'Is that Qusay Hussein at the omelet bar?' / 'Look at this. These guys move, they don't get paid.'
jokes.setup: Family watches what seems like a lavish 40th anniversary party
jokes.punchline: The party featured Qusay Hussein as a guest — revealing the Bluth family's corrupt international dealings — and the workers weren't being paid, suggesting illicit labor practices
jokes.explanation: Densely packed comedy — a single party scene reveals Bluth corruption (Qusay Hussein), unpaid labor, and casual obliviousness to all of it. The specificity of 'Qusay Hussein at the omelet bar' is darkly hilarious. Rewatch bonus for the season context.

jokes.text: George Michael's home video turns out to be a Star Wars reenactment — narrator: 'as a 13-year-old, George Michael had been heavily influenced by the Star Wars films and had endeavored to recreate his favorite moments'
jokes.setup: The family gathers to watch what appears to be a significant home video
jokes.punchline: It's a low-budget Star Wars reenactment filmed by a dorky 13-year-old George Michael with a lightsaber
jokes.explanation: A visual gag that works through accumulation — the buildup of family watching, Michael's horror, and then the payoff at the end of the episode when Ann finds it endearing. Sets up a runner. The content itself is a reliable 'embarrassing nerdy kid' joke elevated by the show's investment in George Michael's arc.

jokes.text: George Sr. reacts to the Star Wars video: 'I spent $2.1 million of company money and end up with this?'
jokes.setup: The family watches George Michael's dorky Star Wars video, expecting embarrassment
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s objection is not the content but the return on investment — revealing he embezzled $2.1 million to fund a party featuring Qusay Hussein, and THIS is what he got
jokes.explanation: Perfectly character-specific — George Sr.'s only concern is the corporate financial loss from a children's party, not the adorable/embarrassing content. The number ($2.1 million) makes it funnier by being specific. Also quietly furthers the criminal subplot.

jokes.text: Buster videotaped himself recreating moments from Chicago — then also liked Star Wars — visual of Buster in a full dance costume, then presumably with a lightsaber
jokes.setup: Someone says even worse videos exist; narrator sets up Buster's tape
jokes.punchline: Buster recreated Chicago dance numbers — deeply specific and absurd — AND Star Wars, the 'he also liked Star Wars' delivered as a deadpan afterthought
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout moments. The Chicago reveal is already absurd and deeply Buster-specific (his arrested development, his mother's dominance, his theatrical helplessness). Then 'He also liked Star Wars' lands as a perfectly timed, tonally flat afterthought that doubles the absurdity. The two-beat structure is textbook AD: unexpected specificity followed by a deadpan capper. High character integration — this joke is unrepeatable with any other character.

jokes.text: His self-esteem is low enough as it is, and I have no idea why. He's such a great kid, you know. And everybody loves him. He's George Michael.
jokes.setup: Michael talks about George Michael's low self-esteem
jokes.punchline: His genuine bafflement that everyone loves a kid named 'George Michael' — oblivious to the social liability of sharing a name with a pop star — is the joke, though the show trusts the audience to see it without spelling it out
jokes.explanation: The name joke is a running gag throughout the series. Michael's sincere pride in the name 'George Michael' — delivered without irony — is the gap between character and audience awareness that drives the humor. Functional but not a peak moment.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'He also liked Star Wars.' — after showing Buster's elaborate Chicago dance video
jokes.setup: Buster's Chicago recreation has just been shown as an example of something potentially lamer than George Michael's Star Wars video
jokes.punchline: The narrator casually adds that Buster also liked Star Wars, implying there's MORE footage — and inviting the audience to imagine it
jokes.explanation: Five words that earn an outsized laugh through pure economy and implication. The deadpan matter-of-fact delivery, the devastating implication of more footage, and the perfect narrative structure (counterpoint collapsed into its own punchline) make this one of the episode's tightest jokes. The audience has to do the imaginative work, which makes it land harder.

jokes.text: Unless he knocks her up like you did with his mother. Plus, she's religious. That one gets pregnant, it stays pregnant.
jokes.setup: Michael says he's not crazy about Ann because she's religious and he hopes George Michael will move on
jokes.punchline: Lucille casually invokes pregnancy as a relationship trap and then makes a dark joke about religious women not getting abortions
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Lucille — cold, calculating, darkly practical. 'That one gets pregnant, it stays pregnant' is a withering encapsulation of her worldview. The follow-up 'Believe me, I dated a chick like that once in high school / No, I didn't' adds a self-correction that implies something even darker about Lucille's past.

jokes.text: Lucille's self-correction: 'Believe me, I dated a chick like that once in high school. / No, I didn't.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is elaborating on her warning about religious girls
jokes.punchline: She begins to claim personal experience dating a religious girl in high school, then immediately retracts it — the retraction implying she's hiding something more interesting than the claim itself
jokes.explanation: A genuinely exceptional joke. The structure is a perfect trap: the audience processes the surprising claim (Lucille dated a girl?) and then before they can react, she retracts it — leaving the implication hanging. The retraction IS the joke. The brevity is surgical. Could only be Lucille — her iron control over her own narrative, mid-slip. High quotability, high rewatch bonus because the implication deepens on reflection.

jokes.text: Ann convinces George Michael to run: 'George Michael, I want you to do it.' / 'But I don't think I'm ready.' / 'Of course you are.' / 'I'm going to lose it before I start.' — all misread as sexual innuendo before 'Don't start. Smoking.'
jokes.setup: Ann and George Michael's dialogue is staged to sound like they're discussing losing George Michael's virginity
jokes.punchline: 'Don't start. Smoking. It's a killer.' — the conversation was about smoking all along, with Ann delivering an anti-smoking speech
jokes.explanation: Classic double-entendre misdirection, executed cleanly. The line readings and word choices ('I'm going to lose it before I start') are carefully chosen to sustain the misread. The reveal — anti-smoking, not virginity — is perfectly absurd given Ann's character. The Sammy Davis Jr. non-sequitur adds another layer of odd charm.

jokes.text: Michael claims to have been student body president; it emerges he lost — 'Who remembers?' / 'I know I would remember.' / 'I didn't say I won. I said I can't remember.' / 'Good memory.'
jokes.setup: Michael encourages George Michael by saying he was student body president himself
jokes.punchline: He progressively reveals he didn't win — retreating from claim to obfuscation to 'I can't remember' while Ann's 'Good memory' closes it perfectly
jokes.explanation: A masterfully escalating exchange. Each retreat from Michael's original claim is funnier than the last, and Ann's final 'Good memory' is a perfectly timed deadpan close from a character usually treated as the butt of the joke. The structure mirrors the episode's election theme. 'Who remembers?' as denial is great character writing.

jokes.text: Michael's account of losing the election: 'the other guy won the actual title, although there were plenty in the school that wanted to overturn the decision. I said, Let's not put the school through that.'
jokes.setup: Michael is explaining why he doesn't remember whether he won
jokes.punchline: He lost but retroactively reframes his concession as a noble sacrifice for the school — transparently self-serving revisionism played completely straight
jokes.explanation: Michael's self-justifying narrative — that he CHOSE to lose for the greater good — is a perfect encapsulation of his character. The language ('I didn't want to put the school through that') echoes presidential concession speeches, making the high-school election both satirize politics and reveal Michael's ego. Character-specific and efficiently delivered.

jokes.text: Ann's ecstatic description of their future wedding night: 'that first time, after waiting and waiting, it's gonna be so awesome! Because it's not just gonna be my love and George Michael's, but God's love, as well. God, it's gonna be incredible!'
jokes.setup: Michael mentions not rushing a physical relationship; Ann eagerly agrees they're both waiting
jokes.punchline: Ann's increasingly enthusiastic description of their eventual first time — invoking 'God's love' in the bedroom with mounting excitement — is deeply uncomfortable and absurd, culminating in 'God, it's gonna be incredible!'
jokes.explanation: Ann's character comedy reaches a peak here. Her religious fervor applied to sexual anticipation creates maximum cringe — it's earnest, weird, and escalating. 'God, it's gonna be incredible!' with the double meaning of invoking God in an exclamation is the perfect button. The joke is that she's excited in a way that's simultaneously devout and accidentally sensual. Deeply character-specific — this is ONLY Ann.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'All right, I'll do it! I'm in.' — immediately misread as a response to Ann's sex speech before clarifying 'Campaign. I'm pumped.'
jokes.setup: Ann has just delivered an ecstatic speech about their future wedding night
jokes.punchline: George Michael's 'All right, I'll do it!' sounds like he's agreeing to sex before he clarifies it's about the campaign
jokes.explanation: A quick callback to the episode's earlier virginity misdirection. Not as well-constructed as the first iteration since the audience now expects the device, but 'I'm pumped' landing after the sex implications still earns a laugh. The word 'pumped' adds a small extra layer.

jokes.text: Buster using a hook hand to do a puzzle: 'Oh, God. I can't put the aorta in with this thing.' / Buster yells dramatically: 'I'm sorry I'm an inconvenience to you, Mother.'
jokes.setup: Buster lost his hand to a seal; he's trying to manage daily tasks with his hook
jokes.punchline: He's doing a heart puzzle — a darkly ironic detail — and his dramatic self-pity about being an inconvenience escalates his suffering into theatrical martyrdom
jokes.explanation: The hook hand gag is well-established; this particular iteration adds the ironic detail of an aorta puzzle (he's doing a heart! with a hook!) and Buster's characteristic over-dramatic martyrdom. Solid but not the best hook hand joke in the series.

jokes.text: Lupe feels sorry for Buster's loss of a hand; Lindsay feels sorry for her loss of Lupe — visual of Lindsay dramatically mourning without a housekeeper
jokes.setup: Lupe has sympathy for Buster's physical disability
jokes.punchline: The narrator equates Lindsay's loss of her housekeeper with Buster's loss of a hand — treating Lindsay's trivial inconvenience as equivalent to dismemberment
jokes.explanation: The narrator's parallel construction — Lupe's sympathy for Buster / Lindsay's equivalent grief — is a small gem of comedic juxtaposition. The structural joke here is entirely in the edit and the narrator's framing, not the dialogue. Perfect economy from the show's signature narrator-as-ironist technique.

jokes.text: Lindsay cleaned one room and now has a garbage problem — visual of her trying to hide garbage bags and accidentally finding George Sr. in the attic
jokes.setup: Lindsay finally cleaned something; now she has garbage to dispose of
jokes.punchline: Her search for a garbage spot leads her to the attic where a fugitive is being hidden — comic escalation from domestic laziness to federal crime
jokes.explanation: Nicely constructed situational escalation — Lindsay's mundane domesticity collision with the season's main criminal subplot. Rewatch bonus for the layered irony: the one time Lindsay attempts housework, she uncovers a federal fugitive.

jokes.text: Lindsay finds Polly the parrot (George Sr.'s only friend in the attic) and thinks of eBay: 'I haven't seen this in ages. Ebay!' — narrator: 'Unfortunately, it was a new friend to George, Senior. Polly!'
jokes.setup: Lindsay finds old items stashed in the attic; George Sr. is hiding there with a parrot
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's delight at finding something to sell on eBay (the parrot) is George Sr.'s only companion being taken away — the cut between her commercial excitement and his desperate cry of 'Polly!' is the joke
jokes.explanation: The joke is built on the gap between Lindsay's oblivious greed and George Sr.'s genuine isolation. 'Polly!' is a great button — the parrot named Polly (a classic joke in itself), and his desperate attachment to a bird as his only friend is both funny and darkly sad. The structural parallel works perfectly.

jokes.text: Gob films office magic: transforming '10:45 in the morning' into lunch time — then producing a live chicken in a meeting
jokes.setup: Gob has been given the camera and arrives at a serious business meeting to film his magic video
jokes.punchline: He disrupts a formal investor meeting with a magic trick that conjures a chicken — derailing the vote Michael needed
jokes.explanation: Classic Gob chaos. His inability to separate personal projects from professional responsibilities (and his total obliviousness to context) is well-drawn. The chicken is a good physical gag and ties to Gob's specific magic iconography.

jokes.text: Michael tries to fire Gob but doesn't know how to call security: 'How do you call security on this thing?' / 'You hit pound-0-0.' / 'Pound is tic-tac-toe, right?'
jokes.setup: Michael fires Gob and tries to have security remove him
jokes.punchline: The authority of the firing dissolves as Michael can't figure out how to work his own phone — Gob has to walk him through it while being fired
jokes.explanation: The joke is structural: the power dynamic of the firing collapses as Michael needs his fired employee's help to complete the firing. 'Pound is tic-tac-toe, right?' is a specific and believable moment of phone confusion that sells the bit. The ending — Gob coaching him through it — is a great reversal.

jokes.text: The security guard who escorts Gob out 'had recently lost half his tie to office magic.' / Guard: 'Oh, I don't mind.'
jokes.setup: The security guard arrives to remove Gob from the office
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals the guard's tie was already half-destroyed by Gob's magic — and the guard's cheerful 'Oh, I don't mind' suggests he's relieved to see Gob go
jokes.explanation: A tiny perfect runner. The visual of a guard with half a tie tells a whole story of Gob's prior office chaos without a word. 'Oh, I don't mind' delivers the guard's full characterization in three words. AD at its most efficient.

jokes.text: Gob filming his own removal: 'Get this. Are you getting this? Cheat out. You have to cheat out a little.'
jokes.setup: Gob is being escorted out of the building by security
jokes.punchline: He treats his own firing/removal as a scene to be filmed, directing the cameraman during his own humiliation
jokes.explanation: Gob's narcissism reaches a new expression: he's so committed to filming his magic video that he sees his own ejection as content. 'Cheat out' (a real filmmaking term for actors adjusting their eyeline toward camera) is a specific detail that rewards film-savvy audiences. Deeply Gob.

jokes.text: Michael to Maeby: 'Your Uncle Gob doesn't not-work here anymore.'
jokes.setup: Michael is explaining to Maeby that Gob has been fired
jokes.punchline: The double negative 'doesn't not-work here anymore' is Michael's tangled attempt to be accurate (Gob never really worked) while still firing him — creating a grammatical and logical knot
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable lines. The double negative perfectly captures the absurdity of Gob's employment status and Michael's exasperation with it. Economy is excellent — eight words do the work of a paragraph.

jokes.text: Maeby learns about her parents' separation from Michael by accident and says: 'Maybe I should just start keeping some secrets of my own.'
jokes.setup: Michael accidentally reveals Tobias moved out; Maeby is upset neither parent told her anything
jokes.punchline: Maeby's threat to 'keep secrets' is darkly ironic given that she's already been secretly posing as a movie executive for the whole season — the joke is entirely in the dramatic irony
jokes.explanation: The comedy is entirely in the dramatic irony for attentive viewers — Maeby is already the most secret-keeping character on the show. Rewatch bonus because the gap between Maeby's complaint and reality is funnier the more you know.

jokes.text: Maeby to Michael: 'Marry me!' then immediately: 'I'm so sorry. I forgot who I was talking to.'
jokes.setup: Michael tells Maeby he's sorry he let something slip accidentally
jokes.punchline: Maeby reflexively says 'Marry me!' — her standard response to being charmed by a man — then catches herself because Michael is her uncle
jokes.explanation: A strong running gag deployment. The immediate self-correction — and its specific reason — is the payoff. 'I forgot who I was talking to' implies she uses 'Marry me' so automatically with men that she has to consciously filter for relatives. Dark undertone, perfect economy.

jokes.text: Ann is using the Bluth company copier to make campaign posters for George Michael — Michael asks 'How do you know Steve Holt? Are you in AA?'
jokes.setup: Ann mentions Steve Holt; Michael assumes she knows him from AA (Alcoholics Anonymous)
jokes.punchline: The assumption that a religious teenage girl would know someone from AA is both a commentary on Michael's biases and an absurd non-sequitur — the joke is that AA is his first explanation for how teens know each other
jokes.explanation: A decent joke that doesn't quite reach the show's best. The AA assumption is funny but not especially character-specific to Michael — it could be said by several characters. The misdirection is mild.

jokes.text: Steve Holt has won 'three years in a row. Every year after his first junior year.'
jokes.setup: Ann describes Steve Holt as the most popular guy in school who wins every election
jokes.punchline: The parenthetical 'every year after his first junior year' reveals Steve has repeated 11th grade multiple times — he's a much older student among teens
jokes.explanation: A perfectly embedded throwaway that rewards careful listening. The information is delivered without emphasis — 'every year after his first junior year' — and the implication (he's been in high school for years) unfolds in the listener's mind a beat later. AD at its best: the joke trusts the audience completely.

jokes.text: 'That's like comparing apples and some fruit nobody's ever heard of.' / 'He might even finish after the Indian kid.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Steve Holt is more popular than George Michael
jokes.punchline: Ann's 'apples and some fruit nobody's ever heard of' idiom variant, followed by George Michael potentially placing third behind 'the Indian kid' — compounding the insult
jokes.explanation: The idiom mutation is sharp — 'apples and some fruit nobody's ever heard of' elevates 'apples and oranges' into something funnier by replacing the recognizable fruit with anonymous obscurity. The double-blow structure (terrible comparison + might lose to third place) is efficient escalation.

jokes.text: After Michael reluctantly agrees to go to the school, Ann hands him campaign posters to put up: 'And do you think you could put up a couple of these when you're there? 'Cause I was really not in the mood to go down there.'
jokes.setup: Ann has been asking Michael to help George Michael's campaign
jokes.punchline: Having leveraged Michael into going to school, Ann reveals she was using him to avoid going herself — a reversal where the supposedly passive Ann has just outsourced her campaign work
jokes.explanation: A small but satisfying character reversal. Ann's blandness conceals a surprisingly effective manipulator — this is a seed of the more active role she takes later in the episode. Not a big laugh but a rewarding character beat.

jokes.text: Lucille fires Lupe: 'You're fired!' / Buster: 'You can't fire me, I'm your son.' / Lucille: 'I'm firing Lupe.' / Buster: 'Well, that makes more sense.'
jokes.setup: Lucille says 'You're fired!' — Buster assumes it's directed at him
jokes.punchline: Buster's protest ('You can't fire me, I'm your son') and his resigned acceptance that being fired as an employee makes more sense than being fired as a son reveals his complete resignation to his mother's dominance
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed three-beat joke. The misunderstanding, Buster's protest, Lucille's clarification, and then Buster's 'Well, that makes more sense' is a complete comedic argument in four lines. The final line is the key: Buster accepts being fired by his mother as a MORE reasonable outcome than not being fired — perfect characterization of their relationship. High character integration and economy.

jokes.text: 'I no does Buster anymore.' / Lindsay: 'Fine. I'll Dustbuster.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay asks Lupe if she can at least come over to Dustbuster or something
jokes.punchline: Lupe refuses, saying 'I no does Buster anymore' — a pun on 'Dustbuster' vs. 'Buster' (Lucille's son) — and Lindsay, missing the pun, takes 'Dustbuster' literally as a verb
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best wordplay jokes. Lupe's 'I no does Buster anymore' functions simultaneously as refusing to clean (Dustbuster = vacuum verb) AND refusing involvement with Buster (the man), while Lindsay hears only the literal cleaning meaning. The economy is extraordinary — five words carry multiple meanings and land as a perfect button. Rewatch bonus because the Buster/Lupe implication is funnier with context.

jokes.text: Tobias calls from the fake TV set bathroom: 'Hey, I can't talk loudly. I'm in the bathroom of the Wrench set and they've started shooting. Also, I don't think this is a real toilet.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has been living on a TV set; he calls Lindsay
jokes.punchline: He's discovered mid-call that the toilet in his 'apartment' is a prop — the casual 'also' making it a secondary concern after the filming schedule
jokes.explanation: A payoff to the absurd setup of Tobias living on a TV set. The implication of what Tobias has been doing with a prop toilet is horrifying and funny in equal measure. 'Also, I don't think this is a real toilet' is a perfect afterthought — the casual 'also' makes it funnier by treating a significant discovery as secondary news. High quotability.

jokes.text: Tobias, mid-call, says a line from the TV show he's on set for: 'You're a dirty cop, so Frank Wrench is going to clean you.' — then is discovered on set.
jokes.setup: Tobias is on the phone with Lindsay while hiding in the bathroom of a TV set during filming
jokes.punchline: He speaks a line of the in-show TV dialogue mid-conversation — collapsing the boundary between his real life and the fictional TV show he's secretly living inside
jokes.explanation: A meta joke about Tobias accidentally method-acting his way into a TV show. The line is amusing but the setup is better than the specific payoff here — it's the concept that's funny more than the specific line chosen.

jokes.text: George Michael's campaign poster — 'We're going to use your idea, and really highlight being chaste.' — the poster is shown to be deeply weird/explicitly religious in content
jokes.setup: Michael suggests the campaign shouldn't focus on the virgin angle
jokes.punchline: Ann has already made a poster highlighting abstinence/Christianity that Michael can see is going to be disastrous — his horrified reaction to the poster is the visual beat
jokes.explanation: The joke rests on Michael's visible horror at the poster and the gap between Ann's earnestness and how the poster will be received. Works as visual comedy and character beat, but we don't see the poster itself in transcript form, limiting its score.

jokes.text: Gob to Lucille: 'Guess who's been fired.' / Lucille: 'Michael fired you?' / Gob: 'No, I fired him. But as a result, I'm no longer welcome in the office.'
jokes.setup: Gob arrives at Lucille's having been removed from the office
jokes.punchline: Gob's reframing — he fired Michael, therefore he's 'no longer welcome' — is pure face-saving revisionism that immediately collapses under its own logic. 'As a result' is the key: he acknowledges the causality while refusing the narrative
jokes.explanation: A superb Gob self-delusion joke. 'No, I fired him. But as a result, I'm no longer welcome in the office' is perfect logic-twisting — he accepts the factual outcome while insisting on his own narrative. The 'as a result' is the comedic engine: he knows what happened but refuses to name it correctly. Could only be Gob. High quotability.

jokes.text: Lucille has replaced Lupe with a Roomba robot vacuum: 'It's a robot. I hate it. I miss Lupe.'
jokes.setup: Lucille says she's found a replacement for Lupe
jokes.punchline: She's bought a robot vacuum, hates it, and immediately misses Lupe — her emotional attachment to a housekeeper revealed through the failure of the replacement
jokes.explanation: Three sentences: reveal, reaction, admission. Perfectly economical. Lucille's cold exterior cracking slightly for Lupe is a tiny humanizing moment delivered as comedy. The Roomba visual gag (a very early reference to the then-novel product) adds texture.

jokes.text: Gob to Lucille about Lupe: 'I want you to be strong, and you don't need the comfort of an immigrant in Mother's old stirrup pants to make you feel that way.'
jokes.setup: Lucille says she misses Lupe; Gob tries to comfort her
jokes.punchline: Gob's 'comfort' is a bizarre, condescending speech culminating in the specific visual detail of 'Mother's old stirrup pants' — inadvertently revealing what Lupe's work uniform actually looked like
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the specificity: 'Mother's old stirrup pants' as Lupe's work uniform is both darkly funny (the indignity of the job) and tells a whole story about the Bluth household. Gob's condescension makes it worse while he thinks he's being supportive.

jokes.text: Gob feeds/interacts with the Roomba as if it's a pet: 'Oh, you're hungry. There you go.'
jokes.setup: The Roomba is moving around Lucille's apartment
jokes.punchline: Gob anthropomorphizes the robot vacuum, apparently feeding it — possibly dropping something in its path — treating the machine like a pet
jokes.explanation: A quick visual absurdist beat that works off the Roomba's movement. Gob's childlike confusion about the machine is on-brand. 'Oh, you're hungry' is sweetly stupid.

jokes.text: Tobias arrives as 'Mrs. Featherbottom' — a female housekeeper from 'Blackstool' — wearing drag and a British accent
jokes.setup: Lindsay needs a new housekeeper; the agency apparently sends someone
jokes.punchline: It's Tobias in elaborate drag — an explicit parody of Mrs. Doubtfire — with the name 'Featherbottom' (a barely-coded Tobias surname reference) and 'Blackstool' (a dark pun on Robin Williams' character's origin)
jokes.explanation: A big character comedy moment. 'Featherbottom' (Tobias's name phonetically scrambled into something feminine and bird-like) and 'Blackstool' (a crude play on 'Shropshire' or similar British place names, but unmistakably scatological) signal that Tobias has committed fully to this. The narrator's deadpan acknowledgment — 'It was the exact plot of Mrs. Doubtfire' — is the perfect self-aware button. High visual impact.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'It was the exact plot of the film Mrs. Doubtfire. There was also some Mary Poppins in there.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has arrived in drag as Mrs. Featherbottom, positioned to infiltrate his own family
jokes.punchline: The narrator completely abandons any pretense of the show being original, flatly acknowledging two films being plagiarized and adding 'There was also some Mary Poppins in there' as a dry bonus
jokes.explanation: A genuinely great meta joke. The narrator's willingness to break the fourth wall and simply say 'this is exactly Mrs. Doubtfire' is more honest and funnier than trying to disguise the reference. 'There was also some Mary Poppins in there' as a throwaway addition is the perfect capper — it acknowledges complexity in the plagiarism. Maximum economy, high originality in the meta-comedic approach.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I'm not that perfect. I pushed George Michael into running for office and I think he's going to get crushed by this kid Steve Holt.' / Lindsay: 'Yeah, I'd vote for him.' / Michael: 'Well, too bad Dad's on the lam.'
jokes.setup: Michael and Lindsay are discussing parental failures
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's immediate readiness to vote for the hot kid over her own nephew, and Michael's casual 'too bad Dad's on the lam' — normalizing a fugitive father as a voting inconvenience
jokes.explanation: Two quick character beats: Lindsay's shallow vote and Michael's matter-of-fact fugitive reference. Both work as character comedy but the scene doesn't build to anything exceptional — it's connective tissue that earns decent laughs through character specificity.

jokes.text: George Sr. has a religious awakening in the attic and tells Michael: 'Our Heavenly Father let his son die so that our sins could be absolved.' / Michael: 'Great, so you're a Christian now.'
jokes.setup: Michael goes to ask George Sr. for help with dirty political tricks
jokes.punchline: George Sr. — a man who committed massive fraud and is hiding from the law — has had a religious awakening; Michael's deadpan 'Great, so you're a Christian now' deflates it perfectly
jokes.explanation: Michael's flat dismissal of his fugitive criminal father's sudden Christianity is a great deadpan beat. The irony is layered — George Sr. citing 'letting his son die' to a son he's asking for help while hiding is rich, but it's Michael's 'Great, so you're a Christian now' that lands the laugh.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I'll call the warden for you. You can ask him yourself.' / George Sr.: 'Well, I think that's for fresh crimes.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. says he needs to seek forgiveness; Michael suggests calling the warden
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s logic that divine forgiveness is 'for fresh crimes' while his existing crimes are too established — casual acceptance of his criminality as a fixed condition
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s casual legal taxonomy — 'fresh crimes' vs. established ones — is darkly funny character comedy. His matter-of-fact acceptance of being a criminal while still parsing the spiritual mathematics of forgiveness is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: George Sr. says Michael and Gob were like 'those biblical brothers, Gallant and... Goofuth.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is having a religious awakening and draws a biblical parallel between Michael and Gob
jokes.punchline: 'Gallant and Goofuth' — a made-up biblical name that sounds like 'Goofus,' from the children's comic 'Highlights' magazine, mixed with biblical naming conventions — George Sr.'s religious knowledge is clearly makeshift
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'Goofuth' perfectly captures George Sr.'s improvised religiosity — he's cobbling together Highlights magazine (Goofus & Gallant) with biblical name suffixes. The joke rewards cultural literacy (knowing Goofus & Gallant) while working on pure sound alone ('Goofuth' sounds inherently funny). George Sr.'s complete confidence in his invented biblical name is the final touch. High originality, high quotability, strong character integration — this is the kind of specific, layered joke AD is celebrated for.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Michael realized that he had lived like Gallant, and if he wanted some dirty tricks to help his son's election, he would have to find Goofuth.'
jokes.setup: 'Goofuth' has just been introduced as Gob's biblical parallel
jokes.punchline: The narrator adopts 'Goofuth' as if it's an established term, using it as Gob's actual name in the narration — fully committing to George Sr.'s made-up mythology
jokes.explanation: The narrator's immediate adoption of 'Goofuth' as legitimate terminology is a perfect comedic commitment. It doubles the joke — the first time is George Sr.'s invention, the second time is the show's narrator treating it as real. The structure of 'lived like Gallant / would have to find Goofuth' is elegant.

jokes.text: Michael apologizes to Gob and manipulates him into helping: 'The guy that he's running against is basically a young you.' / Gob: 'Wow. Won't be easy to win a race against myself. Of course, if anyone can do it, I can.'
jokes.setup: Michael needs Gob's help running George Michael's campaign against Steve Holt
jokes.punchline: Gob's narcissism processes the opponent being 'a young him' as simultaneously a compliment (Steve is great-looking!) and a challenge (he must compete against his own greatness), concluding with self-congratulatory confidence
jokes.explanation: 'Won't be easy to win a race against myself. Of course, if anyone can do it, I can' is a perfect Gob spiral — his narcissism trapping him in a logical loop that he resolves through more narcissism. The construction is elegant: the paradox set up and immediately resolved by Gob's unwavering self-belief.

jokes.text: Michael: 'He just needs to beat the Indian kid.' — treating third place as the realistic campaign goal
jokes.setup: Michael is briefing Gob on the campaign strategy
jokes.punchline: The campaign's entire ambition has been downgraded from 'win' to 'beat one specific person' — revealing how low George Michael's prospects actually are
jokes.explanation: A callback to Ann's earlier assessment, now adopted as official campaign strategy. The joke is in the resigned specificity — not 'win' but 'beat the Indian kid' — which tracks with the episode's running thread of spectacularly lowered expectations.

jokes.text: Michael: 'The mere fact that you think you can says to me that I shouldn't let you. Of course, we both need the work.'
jokes.setup: Gob says confidently 'if anyone can do it, I can'
jokes.punchline: Michael's logic: Gob's confidence is itself evidence he should NOT be trusted, but financial necessity overrides good judgment. The self-aware concession 'we both need the work' is a rare Michael moment of honest pragmatism
jokes.explanation: Michael's self-aware analysis — 'his confidence is the problem' — followed immediately by capitulating anyway is a quietly excellent bit of character writing. It acknowledges Michael's intelligence while showing his compromise. The structure is sharp.

jokes.text: Lucille yells for 'Robot' (the Roomba) to answer the door: 'Robot'll get it. Where the hell is my maid? Robot!'
jokes.setup: Lucille has replaced Lupe with a Roomba
jokes.punchline: She's treating the Roomba as a fully functional servant, calling it 'Robot' and expecting it to answer the door
jokes.explanation: Lucille calling the Roomba 'Robot' as if it's a servant's name is a solid runner. Her complete inability to accept the Roomba's limitations as a door-answering butler captures her character well.

jokes.text: Buster is vacuuming with the Roomba strapped to his hook arm: 'What do you expect, Mother? I'm half machine! I'm a monster!'
jokes.setup: Lucille calls for 'Robot' to do chores; the Roomba is elsewhere
jokes.punchline: Buster has attached the Roomba to his hook hand — becoming a cyborg vacuum cleaner — and is having an existential crisis about being 'half machine'
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke. Two running gags from the episode (Buster's hook hand and Lucille's Roomba) collide to create something entirely new: Buster as a literal man-machine hybrid, having a melodramatic breakdown about his monstrousness. 'I'm half machine! I'm a monster!' is perfectly calibrated Buster — his theatrical self-pity applied to a genuinely absurd physical situation. The visual (a grown man with a vacuum robot attached to a hook arm) is pure AD DNA: specific, escalating, character-rooted. High quotability, high rewatch value, and it works on every level from pure slapstick to character tragedy. This approaches the caliber of the vasectomy anchor joke.

jokes.text: Campaign strategy meeting — Gob suggests handing out condoms labeled 'We salute George Michael': 'We hand out rubbers that say, We salute George Michael.'
jokes.setup: Michael and Gob are planning George Michael's campaign; Gob has ideas
jokes.punchline: Gob's first campaign idea is distributing condoms — a near-perfect inversion of the campaign's Christian abstinence message, and also using 'salute' with condoms creates an implicit crude visual
jokes.explanation: Gob's complete obliviousness to the campaign's Christian messaging, combined with the phallic double meaning of 'salute,' makes this a tight Gob joke. The specific phrasing 'We salute George Michael' as a condom tagline is bizarre and funny.

jokes.text: 'Uh, good point, Two Percent.' / '2%, huh? Is that how little I lost by?' / 'Lost by?' / 'It was close.' / 'That's all you got.'
jokes.setup: Gob dismisses Michael as 'Two Percent' as a jab about his earlier admission
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately interprets '2%' as the margin of his lost election (revealing he does remember details) — Gob's 'That's all you got' is a devastating close
jokes.explanation: A brilliant callback payoff. Michael spent the earlier scene insisting he 'can't remember' whether he won — now '2%' triggers an immediate, precise memory of the exact margin. He DOES remember; he was lying. Gob's 'That's all you got' is a perfect closer that acknowledges the pathos. The structural callback to an earlier scene is executed with economy and character precision.

jokes.text: Ann storms out of the campaign meeting: 'I can't support this.' / Gob: 'And neither can the Christian kids that were going to make you president.' / Michael: 'She's wh... Ann?' / 'I think she's really upset about this.' / 'She'll forgive you. You're George Michael!'
jokes.setup: The campaign team is discussing going negative against Steve Holt; Ann objects
jokes.punchline: Gob's immediate political calculation about the Christian vote, Michael's failure to even recognize Ann at first ('She's wh... Ann?'), and then Gob's confidence that Ann will forgive because 'You're George Michael' — a running joke about his name
jokes.explanation: A multi-beat scene with several smaller jokes: Michael's 'She's wh...' reaction beat (he literally doesn't recognize her), Gob's cold political calculus about Christian voters, and the 'You're George Michael!' name joke runner. Competent ensemble comedy.

jokes.text: Mrs. Featherbottom gives Maeby 'fun-fun' (phen-phen): 'Now take your fun-fun.' / Maeby: 'This is my mom's. And it's Phen-phen. And it's been banned by the FDA.'
jokes.setup: Tobias/Mrs. Featherbottom is acting as the family's nanny
jokes.punchline: He's dosing Maeby with Lindsay's banned diet drug while calling it 'fun-fun' — Maeby has to correct him on both the name and the drug's legal status
jokes.explanation: The joke layers multiple comedic elements: Tobias in drag giving controlled substances to a minor, the FDA-banned drug belonging to Lindsay (character detail), and Maeby's exhausted correction. Dark but well-constructed.

jokes.text: Mrs. Featherbottom sings a song about reading medication labels — a Mary Poppins-style number about a mundane FDA topic
jokes.setup: Tobias is playing the role of a singing nanny (Mrs. Doubtfire/Mary Poppins pastiche)
jokes.punchline: The song is about reading prescription drug labels — a grimly practical topic delivered with full musical theater enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: The gap between the whimsical musical delivery and the mundane/dark subject (FDA-banned diet drugs) is the joke. Tobias's theatrical instincts applied to a deeply inappropriate context is classic character comedy. The specific content isn't quoted at length but the concept is solid.

jokes.text: Tobias as Mrs. Featherbottom: 'I'm sure wherever your father is right now, she loves you very, very much.' / Maeby: 'Not enough to be honest with me.'
jokes.setup: Tobias (disguised as a female housekeeper) is talking to Maeby about her absent father
jokes.punchline: He refers to 'your father' as 'she' — his disguise identity bleeding into his description of himself — while Maeby's response is genuinely cutting and he doesn't realize she's talking TO him
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Tobias accidentally uses 'she' for 'your father' — either a slip of his disguise, a deep ambiguity about his gender identity, or both. Then Maeby's genuinely painful response ('Not enough to be honest with me') hits him directly without him understanding it's aimed at him. The cringe layers perfectly: dramatic irony, gender comedy, and real emotional pain in the same breath. Only possible with these specific characters in this specific situation.

jokes.text: Tobias as Mrs. Featherbottom to Maeby: 'I'm sure wherever your father is right now, she loves you very, very much.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is trying to connect with Maeby while in disguise; he refers to her absent father in third person
jokes.punchline: He accidentally uses 'she' for the father — either because he's slipping out of character or because of his gender presentation issues — and neither of them reacts to the pronoun slip
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest moments. The pronoun slip 'she loves you very, very much' — referring to the father — is either Tobias's subconscious femininity breaking through or him accidentally breaking character, or both simultaneously. The show doesn't explain it; it trusts the audience. Perfect character integration and exceptional rewatch value.

jokes.text: George Michael's campaign video features him as an immigrant child — set in a famine — in Corona del Mar: 'Drought, starvation — these are the things my family has had to deal with for generations, although, fortunately, not since we moved to Corona del Mar.'
jokes.setup: George Michael's campaign video begins with a dramatic portrayal of generational hardship
jokes.punchline: The hardship claim is immediately undercut by the 'not since we moved to Corona del Mar' qualifier — the Bluths are fabulously wealthy, and the qualification destroys any attempt at pathos
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed self-undermining statement. The setup creates the false impression of genuine hardship; the qualifier blows it up. 'Although, fortunately, not since we moved to Corona del Mar' is precisely the right amount of specific (a wealthy Orange County enclave) to maximize the irony. The confidence of the delivery as if it's still a moving claim is perfect.

jokes.text: Audience member yells 'Go home, you terrorist!' at George Michael's immigrant-themed campaign video
jokes.setup: George Michael's campaign video presents him with an immigrant backstory
jokes.punchline: An audience member's immediate, offensive non-sequitur response reveals the campaign video has backfired spectacularly
jokes.explanation: A dark joke about the video's reception, but relies on audience members being casually racist rather than any genuine wit. The character integration is low (it's a nameless audience member). It works as escalating disaster comedy but isn't particularly original.

jokes.text: Gob: 'How stupid do you have to be to say something like that?' — immediately after yelling offensive comments at the screen himself
jokes.setup: An audience member yells 'Go home, you terrorist!' at George Michael's video
jokes.punchline: Gob, who has apparently been making similar or worse comments, criticizes the person for saying it — completely unaware of his own hypocrisy
jokes.explanation: Gob's total lack of self-awareness — immediately criticizing someone for doing exactly what he's been doing — is perfectly Gob. The joke is in the gap between his moral pronouncement and his own behavior, implied by context.

jokes.text: Gob to Michael: 'Sure you didn't make that video in a cave?!'
jokes.setup: George Michael's campaign video has been revealed to be a disaster; Michael confronts Gob about it
jokes.punchline: Gob's criticism of the video is to insult Michael for producing something that looks like terrorist propaganda — again, without noting that he produced it
jokes.explanation: A decent line but relies on post-9/11 'cave' imagery that dates the joke. The Gob hypocrisy is funnier in concept than in this specific line.

jokes.text: Steve Holt's campaign video was produced by Ann Veal — revealed as a surprise
jokes.setup: Ann stormed out of George Michael's campaign over ethics; we expect her to be sidelined
jokes.punchline: Ann immediately pivoted to managing the opposing campaign — her moral objection was less about values than about picking a winner
jokes.explanation: A great character revelation — Ann's principled departure was actually strategic positioning. It retroactively reframes her as more calculating than her bland exterior suggests, which is one of the show's favorite moves with 'background' characters. Rewatch bonus because her 'I can't support this' now reads as negotiation, not ethics.

jokes.text: Steve Holt's campaign video begins — 'Hi, I'm Steve Holt' — and everyone's reaction: 'This kid's popular? / Looks like a chick I did once in high school. / Get a haircut!'
jokes.setup: Steve Holt has been built up as the most popular kid in school
jokes.punchline: Steve Holt looks nothing like a traditional heartthrob — the audience reactions (including Gob recognizing him as resembling someone he 'did') are the joke
jokes.explanation: The subverted expectation (most popular kid doesn't look the part) is enhanced by Gob's recognition line — which will pay off immediately as a major plot point. Gob's 'Looks like a chick I did once in high school' is darkly funny and seeds the revelation.

jokes.text: Steve Holt's video: '...but recently I've been dealing with the fact that I've never known my father. But I finally found a new father. You're probably wondering what these footsteps are. Well, this is my second take. It's kind of like a second chance.'
jokes.setup: Steve's heartfelt campaign video about not knowing his father
jokes.punchline: The video includes an accidental meta-commentary — 'This is my second take. It's kind of like a second chance' — treating a filmmaking slip as profound metaphor, revealing Steve's accidental poetry
jokes.explanation: Steve's accidental profundity — turning 'this is my second take' into a metaphor for second chances — is sweetly absurd. It also seeds the fatherhood revelation. The video is clearly terrible and yet lands better than George Michael's. Rewatch bonus for the Gob connection.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Yeah, okay. We need to pull the videotape.' / Gob: 'My tape? Why?' / Michael: 'I made a huge mistake.' — George Michael's tape has already gone out; Steve Holt's video has revealed he doesn't know his father
jokes.setup: Steve's video reveals he's searching for his unknown father; Michael panics about the attack video
jokes.punchline: Michael's realization that attacking a kid searching for his absent father will look monstrous — the moral panic of a political mistake — while it's too late to stop it
jokes.explanation: A solid escalation beat that drives the plot while being funny. The political instinct ('pull the tape') colliding with too-late realization is functionally comedic but not exceptional as a standalone joke — it's structural comedy.

jokes.text: George Michael's attack video: 'Steve Holt is a bastard. He doesn't even know who his real father is.' — while his video plays, the audience sees Steve Holt's genuine emotion from his own video
jokes.setup: George Michael's negative attack ad runs after Steve's heartfelt video about his unknown father
jokes.punchline: The attack ad accidentally uses the word 'bastard' in the literal sense (unknown father) immediately after Steve's video made his fatherlessness sympathetic — the timing is spectacularly cruel and catastrophic
jokes.explanation: A beautifully timed catastrophic escalation. The word 'bastard' landing immediately after Steve's video about never knowing his father makes it simultaneously cruel and technically accurate — the gap between intent and reception at its comedic peak. The structure is perfect cringe comedy.

jokes.text: George Michael's video: 'His dad is a powerful executive, working for this man.' — followed by footage of George Bluth Sr., a federal fugitive
jokes.setup: George Michael's attack video is trying to make HIM look good by citing his father's connections
jokes.punchline: The 'powerful executive' turns out to be a federal fugitive wanted for fraud and treason — the positive credential instantly becomes a negative one
jokes.explanation: Another self-undermining credential. George Michael's endorsement by association with 'a powerful executive' immediately collapses when the executive is revealed to be a wanted man. Rewatch bonus because of how the season's arc enriches the joke.

jokes.text: George Michael's video: 'The girls like him just fine. Young and old, it doesn't matter... in the dark.'
jokes.setup: George Michael's video is trying to make him seem sexually appealing to voters
jokes.punchline: 'In the dark' as a qualifier to girls liking him regardless of age suggests something much darker — the campaign video has apparently descended into something deeply inappropriate
jokes.explanation: The 'in the dark' qualifier transforms an already-strange boast into something genuinely sinister. Gob's creative vision applied to a high school election produces something that would end careers. Economy is good — the implication is maximally dark for three words.

jokes.text: Gob: 'That's why you had me do that?' — Gob apparently didn't realize the intended purpose of the scenes he shot
jokes.setup: George Michael's attack video has been playing to horrified reactions
jokes.punchline: Gob's question implies he filmed the content under a different pretense — he didn't know what it was for, which raises the question of what he thought he WAS doing
jokes.explanation: A quick reaction beat that implies more than it states. The implication that Gob filmed 'in the dark' footage without understanding its purpose (or understanding it all too well) is funny but underdeveloped.

jokes.text: The Star Wars footage appears mid-attack-video: 'Uh-oh... I couldn't find another tape.'
jokes.setup: George Michael's attack video is already a disaster; there's a further escalation
jokes.punchline: The George Michael Star Wars reenactment footage (from the episode's opening) has been accidentally spliced into the attack ad — the most embarrassing possible addition to an already catastrophic video
jokes.explanation: A masterful callback payoff. The Star Wars footage — established 16 minutes earlier as George Michael's most embarrassing secret — resurfaces in the worst possible context (a political attack ad). 'I couldn't find another tape' is a deadpan explanation that makes it worse. The narrative economy is exceptional: one detail planted at the top of the episode pays off at the climax. Structure is near-perfect — setup, forgetting, devastating return. This earns its high score.

jokes.text: The assembly audience's reaction: 'I liked Steve's more.'
jokes.setup: George Michael's catastrophically embarrassing attack video (ending with Star Wars footage) has just finished
jokes.punchline: An audience member's flat, polite preference for the opposing video is the understatement that summarizes the disaster
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan economy. Four words describing the scale of the campaign disaster with maximum understatement. Character integration is low (anonymous audience member) but the timing and restraint are excellent.

jokes.text: Post-election results: Steve got 'Steve dropped out of the race. He wants to spend time finding his real father.' / Rav Nadir wins with 97% / 'That leaves 3%.' / 'You did better than I did when I ran.' / 'No, I tied with Bart Simpson and School Sucks.'
jokes.setup: The election results are delivered after the video disaster
jokes.punchline: A cascade of humiliations: Steve drops out (making the negative campaign pointless), the Indian kid wins 97%, George Michael gets 3%, Michael claims this is better than his own run, George Michael reveals he tied with protest votes 'Bart Simpson' and 'School Sucks'
jokes.explanation: The episode's climactic joke sequence. Each beat escalates the disaster: Steve's exit makes the campaign pointless; 97% for Rav Nadir is shocking; Michael's consolation ('you did better than I did') is immediately punctured by 'I tied with Bart Simpson and School Sucks.' The protest vote specificity — two culturally perfect protest votes — is the peak of the sequence. Masterful escalating structure that pays off multiple episode threads simultaneously.

jokes.text: As George Michael laments, a kid walks by and says: 'Hey, Star Dork!' / Michael: 'Hey, he knows you now.'
jokes.setup: Michael has been trying to raise George Michael's self-esteem by running for office
jokes.punchline: The only result of the entire campaign is that George Michael is now known as 'Star Dork' — Michael's positive spin on this is simultaneously loving and delusional
jokes.explanation: A perfect closing beat for the episode's main story. Michael's delusional optimism — 'Hey, he knows you now' — as consolation for the worst possible nickname is quintessential AD: the gap between Michael's good intentions and catastrophic outcomes, delivered with his characteristic spin. 'Hey, he knows you now' is one of the episode's most quotable lines.

jokes.text: Ann finds the Star Wars footage endearing and asks George Michael to 'grab a current rod' and come over
jokes.setup: George Michael thinks the Star Wars footage has destroyed his life
jokes.punchline: Ann — the only person who finds it attractive — uses it to invite George Michael over; 'grab a current rod' (a lightsaber) reveals she wants to do Star Wars reenactments with him
jokes.explanation: A sweet and funny payoff for George Michael's arc. Ann's enthusiasm for the lightsaber footage — treating it as attractive rather than embarrassing — is a perfect character note that recontextualizes her. 'Current rod' (a term for lightsaber that Ann apparently knows) reveals her own nerd credentials.

jokes.text: Ann watches George Michael's Star Wars moves and says: 'You were incredible. I've only gotten better.' George Michael casually confirms he's still doing lightsaber choreography
jokes.setup: George Michael is humiliated by the Star Wars video playing at school
jokes.punchline: Ann — his devout religious girlfriend — is genuinely moved by his lightsaber moves; and George Michael admits he's continued practicing — at his age
jokes.explanation: A warm and funny resolution. The joke is that the thing George Michael was most ashamed of is what makes Ann fall more in love with him — and that he's still doing it. 'I've only gotten better' is both dorky and endearing. Perfect character integration for both characters.

jokes.text: Ann: 'So, you want to grab a current rod and go over to my place?' — clearly referring to lightsaber practice equipment; George Michael: 'Wow, I'd love that.'
jokes.setup: Ann has just praised George Michael's lightsaber moves
jokes.punchline: 'Grab a current rod and go over to my place' sounds like a sexual invitation but refers to lightsaber/neon practice equipment — a callback to the episode's sexual misdirection motif with these characters
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the episode's running misdirection gag with Ann and George Michael. 'Current rod' sounds suggestive but is actually a prop weapon. The show has been doing this all episode, and this is a satisfying closing beat on that motif.

jokes.text: Michael watches George Michael and Ann depart and says to himself: 'Got to do something about that kid's self-esteem.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is happy because Ann likes his Star Wars video; Michael has just watched the entire election disaster unfold
jokes.punchline: Michael's conclusion — delivered after a successful resolution for George Michael — is still 'I need to fix his self-esteem,' missing that George Michael is fine and the problem was Michael's own interference
jokes.explanation: A perfect episode button. The line calls back to Michael's opening mission ('I've got to work on that boy's self-esteem') and closes the circle: despite the entire election disaster being Michael's fault, he still arrives at the same conclusion. His inability to see that he's the problem is a key Michael character flaw, delivered with maximum economy. Bookending the episode's theme with the identical sentiment is elegant writing.

jokes.text: Michael watching them leave: 'Got to do something about that kid's self-esteem.' — the very problem he set out to fix, now worse
jokes.setup: Michael began the episode wanting to help George Michael's self-esteem; his intervention has caused humiliation
jokes.punchline: Michael ends up back where he started — George Michael's self-esteem still a problem, now compounded by public humiliation — and Michael hasn't learned anything
jokes.explanation: The closing button perfectly mirrors the episode's opening concern, but Michael has made everything worse and learned nothing. This is classic sitcom structure used with AD's characteristic irony — the hero fails, nothing changes, and he still doesn't get it. Good economy.

jokes.text: Lucille calls to Lupe: 'Yoo-hoo! I'll take you the rest of the way!' — reprising Lindsay's intercepting move from the opening
jokes.setup: Narrator: 'Lucille finds a housekeeper that she won't lose her son to'
jokes.punchline: Lucille is now intercepting Lupe herself — having fired her and then decided she needs her back — with the exact same ruse Lindsay used at the episode's opening
jokes.explanation: A structural callback that rewards the attentive viewer. The exact scenario from the episode's cold open — intercepting Lupe mid-journey — is now reversed and reprised by Lucille. The narrator's 'a housekeeper she won't lose her son to' is a pointed reference to the Buster/Lupe revelation. Perfect structural symmetry and strong character comedy: Lucille adopts Lindsay's exact petty move.

jokes.text: Steve Holt tracks down his real father — the scene cuts to Gob. Steve: 'I've been wondering my whole life who he is. A scientist, a doctor, a senator...' / Narrator: 'Well, we got some bad news.'
jokes.setup: Steve Holt has been searching for his father after his video; he finally finds him
jokes.punchline: His father is Gob — a revelation set up by Gob's 'looks like a chick I did once in high school' reaction. 'We got some bad news' is the perfect deadpan close from the narrator. Steve's list of what his father might be (scientist, doctor, senator) makes the reality of 'Gob' maximally deflating
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke and one of the best in the series. A perfectly planted and executed revelation: Gob's throwaway line ('looks like a chick I did once in high school') was simultaneously a recognition and a parentage confession — paid off here. Steve's aspirational list of what his father might be (scientist, doctor, senator) lands against the cut to Gob with maximum impact. The narrator's 'Well, we got some bad news' is a 10/10 button. The joke rewards attentive viewers across 40 minutes while working as an immediate gut-punch. Everything about this is earned: the foreshadowing, the escalation through Steve's video, the reveal structure. Approaches the vasectomy benchmark.

tmdb_overview: George Michael runs for student body president, which Gob manages to ruin. Buster has an affair with Lupe, who is being sought after by Lindsay and Tobias attempts to move out of the model home, then develops a new persona when he regrets his decision.
insight: Political satire engine misfires—79.5 score masks uneven pacing across scattered character threads.
